transliteration:
guṇān etān atītya trīn dehī deha-samudbhavān
janma-mṛtyu-jarā-duḥkhair vimukto ’mṛtam aśnute
literal_meaning: Having gone beyond these three gunas that give rise to the body, the embodied one is freed from birth, death, old-age and their sorrows, and tastes the deathless nectar.
contextual_meaning: When a person psychologically detaches from the automatic pulls of tamas (inertia), rajas (over-drive) and even sattva (subtle attachment to being ‘good’), the nervous system stops recycling the same core fears of aging, dying and being insignificant. In that open space, a non-ordinary joy—amṛta—becomes an immediate somatic experience, not a post-mortem promise.

shadow: Fear of being 'blank' without familiar drives

psychological_state: Post-traumatic growth meets non-dual awareness: the personality remains intact while the compulsive story-maker is off-line; default-mode-network quietens, and the body registers a safe-enough state to release existential vigilance.
trigger_scenario: A high-performing professional survives a health scare, realizes the script of 'achieve → matter → be remembered' is not mandatory, and begins to feel unexplainable contentment while doing 'nothing special'—gardening, breathing, being with grand-children without multitasking.
anchor_text: Drop the gunas and sip deathless ease right here.
